fact: Four thousand British veterans marched inland from the Chesapeake toward Washington
source: The Burning of Washington: The British Invasion of 1814, Anthony S. Pitch (1998). British force under Major General Robert Ross numbered approximately 4,500 troops.

fact: The American militia line broke and ran at the Battle of Bladensburg
source: The Battle of Bladensburg (August 24, 1814) is widely documented as a rout, sometimes called 'the Bladensburg Races' due to the speed of the American retreat.

fact: Dinner was still warm on the President's table when the British arrived at the White House
source: Eyewitness account of British officer George Robert Gleig and others; documented in The Burning of Washington, Anthony S. Pitch (1998). British officers reportedly found a meal set for 40 guests and ate it before setting the building alight.

fact: The main U.S. regular army was deployed far to the north, fighting in Canada, leaving Washington poorly defended
source: The Civil War of 1812, Alan Taylor (2010). The bulk of U.S. regular forces were committed to the northern theater and campaigns into Upper and Lower Canada, leaving the Chesapeake region defended primarily by militia.

fact: The British walked into an essentially empty city and burned the White House and other public buildings in August 1814
source: White House Historical Association — 'The Burning of Washington'
